tEn: Tony Fadell on Building the iPhone and the Importance of Storytelling
tZh: 托尼·法德尔谈 iPhone 的构建与讲故事的重要性
sEn: Tony Fadell shares insights on building the iPhone, the role of storytelling in product development, and why you need humans in the loop.
sZh: 托尼·法德尔分享构建 iPhone 的见解、讲故事在产品开发中的作用，以及为什么需要人类参与其中。

consensus.en: Start from customer pain, then apply new technology to solve it.
consensus.zh: 从客户痛点出发，再用新技术解决它。

consensus.en: Great products need a singular vision and opinion-based decisions for 1.0.
consensus.zh: 伟大产品需要单一愿景和基于意见的决策来打造1.0版本。

consensus.en: Marketing must tell the story of why, not just what, to connect with customers.
consensus.zh: 营销必须讲述“为什么”的故事，而不仅仅是“是什么”，才能与客户建立联系。

consensus.en: Products need three generations: make it, fix it, then fix the business.
consensus.zh: 产品需要三代：先做出来，再改进，最后优化商业模式。

consensus.en: Even with AI, humans must stay in the loop to avoid technical debt.
consensus.zh: 即使有AI，人类也必须参与其中以避免技术债务。

contrarian.en: Micromanaging key decisions is essential for innovation, not a flaw.
contrarian.zh: 微观管理关键决策对创新至关重要，而非缺点。

contrarian.en: The next iPhone will still need a display; voice-first is the future.
contrarian.zh: 下一代iPhone仍需要屏幕；语音优先才是未来。

contrarian.en: AI code generation creates short-term gain but long-term technical debt.
contrarian.zh: AI代码生成带来短期收益，但造成长期技术债务。

contrarian.en: Hardware plus software startups have more staying power than pure software.
contrarian.zh: 硬件加软件的初创公司比纯软件公司更有持久力。

contrarian.en: Don't chase hype; invest in deep tech before it's hot.
contrarian.zh: 不要追逐热点；在技术变热之前投资深度科技。

contrarian.en: Product builders must consider ethics and avoid addicting users.
contrarian.zh: 产品构建者必须考虑伦理，避免让用户上瘾。

transcript.sec: Introduction of Tony Fadell

turns.en: Today my guest is Tony Fadell. Tony doesn't know this, but ever since I started this podcast, he's been near the top of my wish list of people that I've dreamed to have on this podcast. And that's because Tony is the epitome of what most people listening to this podcast want to become. He co-created some of the most innovative and beautiful and popular products in history. The iPod, the iPhone, the Nest thermostat. He's also famous for being part of the legendary team at General Magic. He's co-authored over 300 patents. He also wrote one of the most important and valuable and inspiring books for builders called Build. Tony is currently an active investor and advisor to deep tech startups with his team at The Build Collective. He was recently named the inaugural designer in residence at the MIT Morning Academy of Design. There's so much gold in this episode. I could go on and on. I'm going to leave it there. Before we get into it, don't forget to check out Lenny's Podcast dot com for a free year of some of the hottest and most well-crafted AI products in the world available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers. With that, I bring you Tony Fadell. Tony, thank you so much for being here and welcome to the podcast.
turns.zh: 今天的嘉宾是 Tony Fadell。Tony 不知道的是，自从我开始做这个播客以来，他一直是我最想邀请的嘉宾名单上的前几位。因为 Tony 正是大多数听众想要成为的那种人。他共同创造了历史上一些最具创新性、最美丽、最受欢迎的产品：iPod、iPhone、Nest 恒温器。他还因曾是 General Magic 传奇团队的一员而闻名。他共同拥有超过 300 项专利。他还写了一本对产品构建者来说最重要、最有价值、最鼓舞人心的书，叫做《Build》。Tony 目前与他的团队在 The Build Collective 活跃地投资和顾问深度科技初创公司。他最近被任命为 MIT Morning Academy of Design 的首任驻校设计师。这一集有太多精华了，我可以一直说下去。我就先到这里。在开始之前，别忘了访问 Lenny's Podcast dot com，获取一些全球最热门、最精心打造的 AI 产品的免费一年使用权，仅限 Lenny 的新闻通讯订阅者。那么，有请 Tony Fadell。Tony，非常感谢你来到这里，欢迎来到播客。

turns.en: Thanks for having me. I appreciate it.
turns.zh: 谢谢邀请，我很感激。

transcript.sec: The BlackBerry vs iPhone Keyboard Debate

turns.en: I have a bazillion questions I want to ask you. I feel like I could fill 4 hours of conversation with all the things that I want to get out of your head. I want to start with the BlackBerry. I was just watching the BlackBerry movie recently and it's kind of this journey of the BlackBerry founders and their story. And then at the end they're like, "Oh, and this iPhone thing launched." And they're like, "Nah, this is dumb. It's like no keyboard. This is not serious. Can't do anything with it." I've always wondered just being on the other side of this, being within Apple building the iPhone, how much did you guys actually doubt that, okay, maybe they have something, maybe we need to add a keyboard?
turns.zh: 我有无数问题想问你。我觉得我可以跟你聊四个小时，把你脑子里的东西都掏出来。我想从黑莓开始。我最近刚看了黑莓的电影，讲述了黑莓创始人的历程和他们的故事。最后他们看到 iPhone 发布，说：“哦，iPhone 这东西发布了。”然后他们说：“不，这很蠢。没有键盘，不严肃，什么都做不了。”我一直好奇，站在另一边，在苹果内部打造 iPhone 时，你们到底有多怀疑：也许他们有点东西，也许我们需要加个键盘？

turns.en: It was the most heated conversation and it dragged out the longest. There was one way of looking at the BlackBerry, which was that is the market we want to go after and we want to win. And then there's the other side, the flip side of that argument, which is only 1% or 2% of mobile phone users at the time had a BlackBerry, knew what a BlackBerry was. So, what about the other 98% of the people? What would they want? What would they need? Why are we going to go after winning this very loyal and incredibly passionate user base and try to pull them away from something. And so, there was this basically head-to-head competition between a display keyboard or virtual keyboard and a physical keyboard. I had been doing virtual keyboards for a while since General Magic and then in the '90s. And I knew what handwriting and keyboards were like on these touch screens. But I was only doing it on a single touch display, resistive or what have you. And so, I knew what the limitations were of those kinds of things. So, I was like, "Hmm, this is really going to be difficult." And we hadn't, you know, multi-touch was just on a big ping pong table. It hadn't been scaled down. So, it wasn't like something in a consumptive form where you could really do user test with it. And so, we set out a set of tests. Like, okay, how fast can I type this text? How can I do this on a hardware keyboard? And then, how can we do this on the virtual one with multi-touch? And it was a hardware-software integration challenge of how we could get this to work. So, we were going back and forth and back and forth. Oh, that doesn't quite work in the software. Oh, that we need to change this in the hardware. And so, this was over a set of months. Would the hardware keyboard be here? And it depends on how much you've been using it, but if there's this margin of error and we could really understand it. This over time was started way down here and then started to get a little faster and a little faster and a little faster. And how many errors? Not just how fast, but how many errors and how do you correct the errors and all of those things. And at the end of the day I was able to convince myself it wasn't going to be a hardware issue. And I was convinced at some point that we were good enough. Were we as good as a hardware keyboard? No. But were we good enough? Yes. And then other people came to that conclusion. But at the same time there were other people who were adamant that the hardware keyboard has to be there. And they were unrelenting. And so it came down to this was a classic, as I say in Build, the data versus opinion based decision. And if you think about it, you had data that said there were pros and cons on both sides. And what happened was the data was not clear that we should choose one over the other. And Steve said, "We are going this way." Enough other people kind of said, "Yeah, that seems like that's the right thing to do."
turns.zh: 那是最激烈的争论，持续的时间也最长。有一种观点认为黑莓是我们想要进入并赢得的市场。但另一方面，当时只有 1% 或 2% 的手机用户拥有黑莓，知道黑莓是什么。那么其他 98% 的人呢？他们想要什么？他们需要什么？我们为什么要去争夺这个非常忠诚、热情的用户群，试图把他们从某样东西上拉走？所以，这基本上是显示键盘或虚拟键盘与物理键盘之间的正面竞争。我从 General Magic 时代，也就是 90 年代，就开始做虚拟键盘了。我知道手写和键盘在触摸屏上的表现。但我只是在单点触摸显示屏上做，电阻屏之类的。所以我知道这类东西的局限性。我当时想：“嗯，这真的很难。”而且，多点触控当时还只是在一个大乒乓球桌上，还没有缩小。所以它还不是一个可以真正进行用户测试的消费形态。于是我们设计了一系列测试：比如，我用这个打字能多快？在硬件键盘上能多快？然后在虚拟键盘上配合多点触控又能多快？这是一个硬件和软件集成的挑战，我们要让它工作。所以我们反复调整：哦，软件上不行；哦，硬件上需要改。这持续了几个月。硬件键盘在这里，取决于你用了多久，但如果有误差范围，我们就能真正理解。随着时间的推移，速度从很低开始，一点点变快。还有错误率——不只是速度，还有错误数量，以及如何纠正错误等等。最终，我让自己相信这不是硬件问题。我在某个时刻确信我们已经足够好了。我们和硬件键盘一样好吗？不。但我们足够好了吗？是的。然后其他人也得出了这个结论。但与此同时，也有其他人坚持必须要有硬件键盘，他们毫不妥协。所以这就像我在《Build》中说的，是数据与意见的经典对决。想想看，数据表明双方都有利弊。结果数据并不明确我们应该选哪个。然后 Steve 说：“我们走这条路。”足够多的人也说：“是的，这似乎是正确的做法。”

transcript.sec: Humans in the Loop and Building for the Long Term

turns.en: You still need humans in the loop. Don't surrender to the machine.
turns.zh: 你仍然需要人类参与其中。不要向机器投降。

turns.en: We can use the machines, but don't cognitively surrender.
turns.zh: 我们可以使用机器，但不要在认知上投降。

turns.en: Because it's so easy to build, the things that stand out are the things that are really well thought through.
turns.zh: 因为构建变得如此容易，脱颖而出的东西是那些经过深思熟虑的。

turns.en: Today in the AI world, I can just make a prompt and all of a sudden it gets spit out. You're building on a really crusty foundation. You're getting short-term gain for very, very long-term loss. If you're going to build a real company, it can't be throwaway.
turns.zh: 今天在 AI 世界里，我只需要写个提示，东西就出来了。你是在一个非常脆弱的基础上构建。你获得的是短期收益，却带来非常非常长期的损失。如果你要建立一个真正的公司，它不能是昙花一现的。

turns.en: One of your colleagues, Hermann Hauser, that ask him how he decides what is worth building.
turns.zh: 你的一位同事，Hermann Hauser，问他如何决定什么值得构建。

turns.en: I always start from pain. Are there new technologies to solve that pain? Bring innovation in, revolution in, and redefine the space.
turns.zh: 我总是从痛点出发。有没有新技术可以解决那个痛点？引入创新，带来革命，重新定义这个领域。

turns.en: What's the threshold? What's the sign that this isn't big enough?
turns.zh: 阈值是什么？什么迹象表明这还不够大？

turns.en: Well, the iPod wasn't big enough. It took three generations of the iPod before it became successful. You got to fail a few times till you find your way.
turns.zh: 嗯，iPod 一开始也不够大。它经历了三代才成功。你得失败几次才能找到自己的路。

turns.en: You are so into marketing, that piece of building that I think a lot of builders don't think about at all.
turns.zh: 你非常注重营销，这是很多产品构建者根本不考虑的部分。

turns.en: The technology's in service of the customer. Not we're going to jam the technology down the customer's throat. A customer only sees what they see through the lens of marketing.
turns.zh: 技术是为客户服务的。不是我们要把技术硬塞给客户。客户只能通过营销的镜头看到他们看到的东西。

turns.en: You often come back to the value storytelling for product builders.
turns.zh: 你经常回到产品构建者的价值叙事上。

turns.en: Too many times when we're technology led, we talk about the what. We don't talk about the why. The why is storytelling. When I watched Steve, he was honing the story of the iPhone every day. And so when you saw him come on stage, it was just because he had done it a hundred thousand times.
turns.zh: 很多时候，当我们以技术为主导时，我们谈论的是“是什么”。我们不谈论“为什么”。“为什么”就是讲故事。当我观察 Steve 时，他每天都在打磨 iPhone 的故事。所以当你看到他上台时，那只是因为他已经练习了十万次。

transcript.sec: Opinion-Based Decisions in 1.0 Products

turns.en: One direction I want to get into here is this idea that you talk a lot about micromanaging being actually really important and powerful. So, there's this image that's floating around Twitter that I don't know if you've seen. I'll show it on the screen as I'm describing it and hopefully you can visualize it. It's a functional system versus a dysfunctional system. Basically, a company that's doing great versus not great. And there's this chart that goes up to more functional that is basically unkind truth and then another unkind truth, another unkind truth creates more functional systems. Kind lie followed by kind lie followed by kind lie leads to dysfunctional systems. Something Steve Jobs is very famous for. Yeah, in your book you talk about mission-driven are actually really You want You want a mission-driven There's certain types of that are great. Talk about just the importance of somebody being very direct in what it takes to build great products.
turns.zh: 我想聊的一个方向是，你经常提到微观管理其实非常重要且强大。Twitter 上流传着一张图，不知道你见过没有。我描述一下，希望你能想象出来。它对比了功能正常的系统和功能失调的系统——基本上就是一家公司做得好与不好。图表显示，走向更功能正常的方向是“残酷的真相”，接着又一个“残酷的真相”，再一个“残酷的真相”，这样会构建出更功能正常的系统。而“善意的谎言”接“善意的谎言”再接“善意的谎言”，则会导致功能失调的系统。这是史蒂夫·乔布斯非常出名的一点。在你的书里，你谈到使命驱动……实际上你希望使命驱动……有些类型的使命驱动很棒。谈谈直接坦率对于打造伟大产品的重要性吧。

turns.en: When you're doing a 1.0 of anything, when you're doing if you're doing anything that matters and it's a 1.0 and it's a new category or it's a new device the world hasn't seen before, you have very few analogs that you can use to make data-driven decisions. And so, if most of your decisions are going to be opinion-based decisions for a 1.0, you have to have one or two or a very, very small set of people who are in charge with making the opinion-based decisions and can actually get you from point A to from a a white paper a you know, a white blank blank sheet of white paper or whiteboard to an actual 1.0 spec. Because if you try to do data-driven decisions all the way along, you're you're either not doing a differentiated product because you're taking data from another thing or you're just getting just data. Right? So, you're going to have to figure out how to get opinion-based decisions to happen, and that means you have to have, you know, for lack of a better word, tastemakers. This is what we are doing. We are the the person or the team who is going to make those opinion-based decisions. Of course, some people aren't going to like it, and it's going to be like, "I'm sorry, this is a benevolent dictatorship. This is what's going to happen, and this is the vision, and we don't know what we don't know until we ship it and we get opinions from the, you know, the the the users." Now, it's very different when you do this on a in a B2B context versus a B2C context. And so, the hardest environment to work with it when when you have these opinion-based decisions is in a B2C context because you have to see these decisions in the full light, and you don't a consumer does. They have to see it in from the marketing, from how they discover it in the marketing, the key uh the key feature sets, the the ability to use the product, all of these different things for them to actually come up with an opinion of what they like and what they don't like and being able to critique it. And if you don't And if you're doing a 1.0 and the world hasn't seen you, you're not going to get that from consumers, ever. You have to ship it, and you have to build the entire kind of ecosystem so those consumers see it in the fullness so that when they do the evaluation and they spend their own money, then you're getting real feedback. When it comes to consumer hard atoms-based products with services or not, whatever it is, you have to build the entire thing and visualize the entire thing to be able to make those uh uh opinion-based decisions. And so, you need a small team who's looking at the marketing angles, the engineering angles, the the sales angles, all those different things to go, "Okay, that's the way we're going." And there's only so many ways you can do it because the rest of the team doesn't see this either. And so, you have to be very articulate about how uh what that opinion-based decision is made for, why, how it might affect market, how you do it, and make sure the team understands it. Now, if the team is just fully against it, well, then maybe it has to be unkind. But hopefully, if you've done a good job and you really, you know, have an informed gut and you can articulate that, you can get everybody moving in the same direction, even if it is and it's going to be risky. You're right, you got to know that you got to take risk, cuz most people in those other functions, they don't want to take any risks. And so, there's someone who's got to be the target, right? And today, you know, in many contexts, people go and hire consultants. Right? They go and hire, okay, we're going to do a user study, or we're going to do all this, and they don't have the user study, they don't have the full context like I was just describing. They don't go buy the product, and they don't what have you, and then they get data. And that's because the leader, or whoever's in there, or board maybe, it's like, we need data to make sure this 1.0 is going to be a success. And I saw this so many times at all these major corporations. So, they're just kind of covering their ass with data, and not really doing the hard work of saying, I'm going to make this decision, and we are going to select this, and yes, I might be wrong, or we as the opinion based decision makers are wrong, and we will correct it later. And we'll take the heat for that.
turns.zh: 当你在做任何产品的 1.0 版本时，如果它很重要，是一个新品类或世界从未见过的新设备，你几乎没有可用来做数据驱动决策的类比。因此，如果 1.0 的多数决策都将是基于观点的决策，你就需要一两个人或一个非常小的团队来负责做出这些基于观点的决策，并且能真正把你从一张白纸或白板带到实际的 1.0 规格。因为如果你全程试图用数据驱动决策，你要么做不出差异化产品（因为你从别处拿数据），要么只是得到数据而已，对吧？所以你必须想办法让基于观点的决策发生，这意味着你需要——用个不太恰当的词——品味创造者。这就是我们在做的。我们就是那个做出基于观点决策的人或团队。当然，有些人不会喜欢，他们会说：“抱歉，这是仁慈的独裁。事情就是这样，这就是愿景，在发布并从用户那里得到反馈之前，我们不知道我们不知道什么。”在 B2B 和 B2C 环境下做这件事非常不同。最难的环境是 B2C，因为你需要让这些决策完全暴露在消费者面前，而消费者不会自己看到全貌。他们必须通过营销、发现方式、关键功能集、产品使用体验等所有方面，才能形成喜欢或不喜欢的意见并做出评价。如果你做的是 1.0 且世界从未见过你，你永远无法从消费者那里得到这些。你必须发布产品，构建整个生态系统，让消费者看到全貌，这样当他们用自己的钱评估时，你才能得到真正的反馈。对于消费类硬件产品（无论是否附带服务），你必须构建并可视化整个产品，才能做出那些基于观点的决策。因此，你需要一个小团队，从营销角度、工程角度、销售角度等各方面审视，然后说：“好，我们就走这条路。”能做的选择有限，因为团队其他成员也看不到全貌。所以你必须非常清晰地阐述这个基于观点的决策是为了什么、为什么、它如何影响市场、你如何执行，并确保团队理解。如果团队完全反对，那也许不得不残酷。但希望如果你做得好，真正有见地的直觉并能清晰表达，你就能让所有人朝同一个方向前进，即使这有风险。你说得对，你必须知道要承担风险，因为其他职能的大多数人不想冒任何风险。所以必须有人成为靶子，对吧？如今在很多情况下，人们会去请顾问。他们会说：“我们做个用户研究吧”，但他们没有用户研究，也没有我描述的全貌。他们不去购买产品，也不做其他事情，然后得到数据。这是因为领导者或董事会说：“我们需要数据来确保这个 1.0 成功。”我在许多大公司见过太多次了。他们只是用数据来保护自己，而不是真正做艰难的工作，说：“我要做这个决定，我们选择这个，是的，我可能错了，或者我们这些基于观点的决策者错了，我们以后会修正，并为此承担责任。”

transcript.sec: Micromanagement as a Leadership Tool

turns.en: So, what I'm hearing here is just the power, especially in a consumer product, of a singular vision of a singular leader that drives it, that is basically relies on their instinct and their taste and their experience.
turns.zh: 所以，我在这里听到的是一种力量，尤其是在消费产品中，一个单一愿景、单一领导者的力量，它基本上依赖于他们的直觉、品味和经验。

turns.en: They and a lot of informed judgment from all the experts around, asking questions, refining, prototyping, these kinds of things to then make a decision. So, it's not just like, I woke up one morning and, you know, this is, you know, now it's we're going this direction. No, it's there's a lot of work to get there.
turns.zh: 他们还需要来自周围所有专家的许多明智判断，通过提问、改进、原型设计等方式来做出决策。所以，这不仅仅是某天早上醒来就说“我们要朝这个方向走”。不，要达到那个目标需要大量的工作。

turns.en: I want to come back to the idea of micromanaging, which a lot of people like to a lot of people that word is a bad. Don't be a micromanager. You advocate for creating great products, you need to actually be really micromanaging. You just speak to that. What do you feel you think miss about the importance and power of micromanaging as a leader?
turns.zh: 我想回到微观管理这个概念，很多人认为这个词是负面的。不要做微观管理者。你主张创造伟大产品需要真正的微观管理。你谈谈这个。你认为作为领导者，人们忽略了微观管理的重要性和力量吗？

turns.en: You know, we've heard the term sweat the details. It's micromanagement of certain details. And then there's the kind of hands-off of other details. You have to really understand the blend of which things really matter, which things don't. When I was early in my career, I thought everything mattered. And I drove everybody nuts, drove myself nuts, the people hated like you know, and it became my it became like everybody's got to do it the way I would do it. It's like no, no, no, no. There's only a few key things mostly for the customer or maybe some certain things for manufacturing or cost or something where it needs to really be very clear or a long-term vision. But then you can delegate other things, but certain pieces of it you really need to And when I mean micromanaging, it means micromanage the decision, not necessarily operations of doing it. Making sure you're getting the data like we did with the keyboard on the iPhone to make sure we're getting the right data to help us get the informed gut to make that opinion-based decision. And so it's the micro specification and delivery of those certain data pieces that you need. And maybe it's also to get out of a crisis. Or maybe it's a system level thing where you have to worry about this thing at the low level changing up here and here and like we could do that, but only if they do this and this is like, "Okay, guys, we're going to fix all of those things at once." And you have to micromanage it because everybody wants to find excuses why they can't do that or they can't do that. So you have to ask why a lot. So it's all in service of some really key detail that needs to get delivered or some innovation that needs to be delivered. Just like I said the keyboard, we had to do the hardware, we had to do the software, we had to do the filtering, you know, we had to do how the graphics were done on the screen. So, you had all of these layers that had to keep constantly changing and adjusting and sometimes you have to micromanage that because there's just too many variables and someone has to be the orchestrator of this huge orchestra of many different components to kind of make it all come together and be harmonious.
turns.zh: 你知道，我们听过“注重细节”这个说法。这是对某些细节的微观管理。而其他细节则要放手。你必须真正理解哪些事情重要，哪些不重要。在我职业生涯早期，我认为每件事都重要。我让所有人都抓狂，也让自己抓狂，人们讨厌这样，结果变成了每个人都必须按我的方式做事。不，不，不。只有少数关键事项，主要是面向客户的，或者某些制造、成本方面的，需要非常明确或符合长期愿景。但其他事情你可以授权，不过某些部分你确实需要亲自处理。我所说的微观管理，是指微观管理决策，而不是执行操作。确保你获得数据，就像我们在 iPhone 键盘上做的那样，确保我们获得正确的数据，帮助我们做出基于直觉的明智决策。所以，这是对你需要的那些特定数据点的微观规范和交付。也许这也是为了摆脱危机。或者是一个系统层面的问题，你必须担心底层的变化会影响到这里和那里，就像“我们可以那样做，但前提是他们这样做”，然后你会说：“好了，各位，我们要一次性解决所有这些问题。”你必须进行微观管理，因为每个人都想找借口说他们做不到这个或那个。所以你必须多问为什么。这一切都是为了交付某个关键细节或创新。就像我说的键盘，我们必须做硬件、软件、过滤，以及屏幕上的图形处理。所以，所有这些层面都需要不断变化和调整，有时你必须微观管理，因为变量太多，必须有人充当这个由许多不同组件组成的庞大乐队的指挥，让一切和谐地融合在一起。

transcript.secZh: 微观管理作为领导工具

transcript.sec: AI needs context: Nest's vision for smart home

turns.en: There was probably a business mismatch. I think if Nest was alive today like it was, it would have a whole different thing with Google and Gemini and Google IO, which was yesterday or the day before. It would have been one of the centerpieces of what you could do because AI needs context. AI needs a lot of context. In a home, you want to make everything seamless. The way you get best context is by having sensors properly placed around the home that don't necessarily invade privacy, but allow you to pick up comings and goings, who's who in the room, your voice, and these kinds of things. Audio, I should say, not voice. To be able to give AIs context so that you can have an anywhere assistant that really knows what's going on.
turns.zh: 可能存在业务上的错配。我认为如果 Nest 像当年那样活到今天，结合 Google 和 Gemini，以及昨天或前天刚结束的 Google IO，它会成为核心亮点之一，因为 AI 需要上下文。AI 需要大量上下文。在家庭中，你希望一切无缝衔接。获取最佳上下文的方式是在家中合理布置传感器，这些传感器不一定侵犯隐私，但能捕捉到人员的进出、房间里谁是谁、你的声音等等。我应该说音频，而不是语音。这样才能为 AI 提供上下文，让你拥有一个真正了解情况的随处可用的助手。

It could have been called that, but we couldn't call it that in 2011 because people would have freaked out. Now, you would have called it the Nest AI Thermostat, right? And people would have bought it. So, we knew what AI was. Since 2010, that was one of the foundational pieces of the company was AI. turns.zh: 哦，当然，当然。人们一直问我，‘我们为什么要做这个？’我说，不，绝对要做。现在就是时机，如果还没发生的话。我实际上收到了很多商业计划书，他们说，‘嘿，这个你感兴趣吗？想投资吗？想过来帮我们搭建吗？这就像 Nest 2.0。’所以 Ring 也在尝试类似的事情。我不认为它非常注重隐私，但那就是他们想做的。说实话，那从一开始就是我们的愿景。我们当时想，‘你在家里需要大量上下文。’因为我们记得，Nest 学习恒温器并不是 Nest AI 恒温器。它本可以那样命名，但我们在 2011 年不能那么叫，因为人们会吓坏。现在，你会叫它 Nest AI 恒温器，对吧？人们会买账的。所以我们知道 AI 是什么。从 2010 年开始，AI 就是公司的基石之一。正是这样我们才能做到很多事，但显然不是大语言模型，而是更小的东西。我们说，‘好吧，我们可以看到这个世界朝着 AI 助手的方向发展。’然后语音助手来了，接着是 Alexa 和 2013、14、15 年发生的那些事。我们说，‘好吧。’那是第一代，但我们能看到这种绽放。不幸的是，随着时间的推移，从 Nest 推出到那个愿景实现花了 15 年多的时间，但现在它就在那里。如果我们当时那样推销 Nest，我们只是太早了。

transcript.sec: How to decide what to build: starting from pain

turns.en: Let me follow this thread around how to know what to build and how you come up with ideas. So you emailed a bunch of people that know you, asked them what to ask you. One of your colleagues, Herman Hauser, his question he wanted me to ask you: ask him how he decides what is worth building.
turns.zh: 让我顺着这条线索，聊聊如何知道该构建什么以及如何产生想法。你给一群认识你的人发了邮件，问他们该问你什么。你的一位同事 Herman Hauser，他想让我问你：问他如何决定什么值得构建。

turns.en: Okay. Well, Herman's great. I've known Herman since 1987, probably before most of your listeners were even born. But Herman, just for context, Herman was the creator of Acorn Computer, which was the Apple II of the UK back in the 70s, and then he created with his team the ARM processor. So Acorn RISC is — ARM means Acorn RISC Machine from Acorn Computer. So he was one of the founders of ARM. I was talking about processors. Anyway, so Herman and I go way back. So the thing is, how do you figure out what is worthy to be built and what's not? The first thing is I start from pain. Some people start from other directions. I always start from pain. That's what I learned: what are people's pain right now, or you can see it on the horizon they're going to have pain in not too far away. But how do you solve for that pain? Typically those pains were because when those products were created, either it was an unintentional consequence or a limitation of the technology at the time, and it kind of just evolved but never revolutionized itself. So it just evolved in that same pain kind of was there, but it gave you enough of a painkiller for the other problem that having this new pain was worth it. So I always start with, okay, where's our current pain and are there new technologies to solve that pain? In the thermostat case, Nest was we could use AI to learn. So it can learn when you're there, when you're away, what temperatures you like, so you don't have to program it, so you can save energy. The big pain was being either comfortable or saving money, because 50% of your energy bill was in this heating and cooling unit that you hated the interface, you didn't know what it was, you just paid the bill. So it started from that pain and said, okay, well, programmable thermostats weren't innovated, but they weren't used. Everyone had them because the energy company would give you rebates, but no one knew how to use it because it was arcane. It was programming a VCR. So what I said was, oh, wait a second, what if it could learn your patterns? And that was AI. So now let's put that together in a much cooler looking, attractive package that cost five to six times more than the things you're buying today. But that was the crazy opinion-based decision: okay, yes, it's going to cost $249, but it's going to save you $800 to $1,200 a year. So it could pay for itself within a year or two. So that was the big idea, and AI was brought to bear on that old problem, and hopefully solve it in a new way. So it starts with the pain, long-time pain, maybe habituated away pain that you have to discover, and new technology bonded with that to bring innovation in, revolution in, and then redefine the space in a way, which is what we did. Not just with the product, but how you installed it — it was always installed by third-party installers as opposed to yourself, how you bought it — you bought it through the installer, not in Best Buy or somewhere else. So we had to reinvent many different pieces of the puzzle to get Nest to be Nest. It wasn't just the product. It was all the other things. Just like the iPod wasn't the iPod, or the iPhone wasn't the iPhone. It was the iPhone plus the App Store, iTunes, iPod plus iTunes, and then the iTunes Music Store. So you have to think about the full thing you're trying to build, not just the one piece, even though that's what you might remember. You have to remember it's a system that you're going to innovate with.
turns.zh: 好的。Herman 很棒。我从 1987 年就认识他了，可能比你大多数听众出生还早。但简单介绍一下，Herman 是 Acorn Computer 的创造者，那是 70 年代英国的 Apple II，然后他和他的团队创造了 ARM 处理器。所以 Acorn RISC 是——ARM 代表 Acorn RISC Machine，来自 Acorn Computer。他是 ARM 的创始人之一。我刚才在聊处理器。总之，Herman 和我交情很深。那么，如何判断什么值得构建、什么不值得呢？首先，我从痛点出发。有些人从其他方向开始，但我总是从痛点开始。这是我学到的：人们现在的痛点是什么，或者你能预见到他们在不久的将来会有的痛点。但如何解决那个痛点呢？通常这些痛点的产生是因为当初创建那些产品时，要么是无意的后果，要么是当时技术的局限，然后它们只是演进，从未自我革命。所以它们在同样的痛点中演进，但那个痛点给了你足够的止痛药，让你觉得忍受这个新痛点是值得的。所以我总是从‘我们当前的痛点在哪里，有没有新技术可以解决它’开始。以恒温器为例，Nest 是我们可以用 AI 来学习。它可以学习你何时在家、何时外出、你喜欢什么温度，这样你就不需要编程，从而节省能源。最大的痛点是要么舒适，要么省钱，因为 50% 的能源账单来自这个供暖和制冷设备，你讨厌它的界面，不知道它是什么，你只是付钱。所以从那个痛点出发，我们说，好吧，可编程恒温器没有创新，但也没人用。每个人都有，因为能源公司会给你返利，但没人知道怎么用，因为它太神秘了，就像编程录像机一样。所以我说，等等，如果它能学习你的模式呢？那就是 AI。那么现在让我们把它包装成一个更酷、更有吸引力的产品，价格是今天你买的东西的五到六倍。但那是一个疯狂的基于直觉的决定：好吧，是的，它要 249 美元，但每年能帮你节省 800 到 1200 美元。所以它一两年内就能回本。这就是大想法，AI 被用来解决那个老问题，希望能以新的方式解决它。所以它始于痛点，长期的痛点，也许是被习惯化而忽略的痛点，你需要去发现，然后新技术与之结合，带来创新和革命，从而重新定义这个领域，就像我们做的那样。不仅仅是产品，还有你如何安装它——以前总是由第三方安装，而不是你自己；你如何购买它——你通过安装商购买，而不是在 Best Buy 或其他地方。所以我们不得不重新发明拼图的许多不同部分，才能让 Nest 成为 Nest。它不仅仅是产品，而是所有其他东西。就像 iPod 不仅仅是 iPod，iPhone 不仅仅是 iPhone。它是 iPhone 加上 App Store、iTunes、iPod 加上 iTunes，然后是 iTunes Music Store。所以你必须考虑你试图构建的整个系统，而不仅仅是一个部分，即使你可能只记得那一个部分。你必须记住，你要用这个系统进行创新。

transcript.secZh: 如何决定构建什么：从痛点出发

transcript.sec: The Two-Part Formula: Pain and New Technologies

turns.en: So, these two-part kind of formula you shared here with pain and new technologies. The second part is really interesting. Essentially, it's like what's the why now? What's like the new tech that has emerged that now allows us to solve this pain? It feels like that's a core part of it. It's interesting because when you talked about the iPhone keyboard, it was a similar story of like okay, we can actually sort of do the virtual keyboard for the first time in history.
turns.zh: 所以，你分享的这个关于痛苦和新技术的两部分公式，第二部分真的很有趣。本质上就是问“为什么是现在”？出现了什么新技术让我们现在能解决这个痛点？感觉这是核心。有意思的是，当你谈到 iPhone 键盘时，也是一个类似的故事：我们终于可以在历史上第一次做出虚拟键盘了。

turns.en: Right, exactly. It could be because of multi-touch. That's really what it was and then we were just on the verge of having fast enough processors, right? And we were just on the verge and so it wasn't you know, if you look at the iPhone or you look Nest iPhone or iPod, you can see where all these technologies were just coming to light. So, in the iPod it was just now mass storage that was portable and battery operated. That was really what it was and it was also MP3s or digital music, right? Those were kind of and we also had high-density we were the first products to have lithium-ion or lithium prismatic polymer cells. So, it was new battery technology, new mass storage portable mass storage, and this new generation of digital music. And we had ARM processors, right? And so and they're really, really low power. So, all of those things had to come together to do that. And then on the iPhone, it was multi-touch, but it wasn't just that, it was also the fact that now we had Wi-Fi everywhere. And we knew that 3G was coming. Because we were it was only 2 and a half G when that was and that was very slow, but we had Wi-Fi. And so, it was And we had cameras, right? Digital cameras. We had the digital camera. We had digital video, which was the we had YouTube at the time. So, it was all of those things just on the verge to say this is what's going to be very different than what just came before it like a BlackBerry, which was really just a texting machine and nothing else.
turns.zh: 对，没错。关键是多点触控。就是这样，而且当时我们刚好有了足够快的处理器，对吧？我们刚好处于临界点。如果你看看 iPhone、Nest、iPhone 或 iPod，就能看到所有这些技术都刚刚崭露头角。在 iPod 上，就是便携式、电池供电的大容量存储。这就是关键，还有 MP3 或数字音乐。我们也是首批使用锂离子或锂聚合物电池的产品。所以，新电池技术、新便携大容量存储，以及新一代数字音乐，再加上 ARM 处理器，功耗非常低。所有这些必须结合在一起。而在 iPhone 上，是多点触控，但不仅如此，还有无处不在的 Wi-Fi。我们知道 3G 即将到来，当时只有 2.5G，速度很慢，但有 Wi-Fi。还有摄像头，数码相机，数码视频，当时还有 YouTube。所有这些都刚刚成熟，让 iPhone 与之前的黑莓截然不同，黑莓基本上只是个发短信的机器。

transcript.secZh: 两要素公式：痛点与新技术

transcript.sec: When an Idea Isn't Good Enough: Threshold for Success

turns.en: I'm going to close the loop on your advice on finding a great idea. I'm curious about the flip side of when it's still not good enough. So, there are so many gadgets out there that are solving some pain level. Maybe there's a new technology that they integrated, but they're still not a big business. It's not a real company. What's the threshold? What's a sign that this isn't big enough?
turns.zh: 我想回到你关于找到好主意的建议上。我很好奇另一面：什么时候还不够好？市面上有很多小工具解决了某种痛点，也许整合了新技术，但还不是大生意，不是真正的公司。门槛是什么？什么迹象表明这还不够大？

turns.en: Well, you know, the iPod wasn't big enough. It took three generations of the iPod before it became successful. Everyone you know, well, anyone who knows you know, if they remember iPod before iPhone because iPhone swallowed everything. It's like the black hole of everything. But, you know, iPod the first generation was only successful with the Mac geeks and the Mac geeks were less than 1% of the market. And then the second generation was also that way. So, when we we get we'd sell everything we'd sell everything we could for the most part in the first quarter and then it would die because it was just the Mac aficionados, the loyalists who'd come and buy everything. And it wasn't until the third generation where we made it work on Windows did it actually start we had the iTunes music store, did it actually start to take off? So sometimes you have to say we're on the right thing, but we need to make some changes to get this market going. And that was a real opinion-based decision. To you know, we the team at the team that I was running, we were really clear that we had to have Windows connectivity out of the gate. Or not out of the gate, but just after the first iPod shipped. And Steve said, "Over my dead body, no way. This is going to help us sell more Macs." So and then he and then you know, we're like, "Okay, we're making the second one. Oh, we're going to keep it with Mac only. We just got to fix a few of these things and then all of a sudden it'll take off." And it was like, it didn't take off. So then it was it's a long story about how that about how we finally got Windows connectivity, but there was always this skunk works thing project behind the scenes doing that. I did the same thing with stylus. Steve never wanted a stylus on the iPhone or the iPad. He never wanted He never wanted it. He's like, "Your finger is good enough. If we can't do it with the finger." It's like And I'm like, "But we're going to have a B2B context and we're going to have forms filling and people are going to write." And and he's like, "I don't care. Just That means we're going to get to like Windows pen." Cuz he thought it was going to be like Windows pen. Which was you had to use the pen for everything as opposed to using your finger. So it was like, I want it instead of pen dominant, I want it finger dominant. But it That's what he was saying. But then when we added the stylus, which was another skunk works project, all of a sudden it came out and was like, "Well, we had to have stylus, right?" And now it's a a big feature of the product. Not everyone uses it, but it is a big feature for certain professionals who who really want that. And artists and hobbyists and stuff. And so sometimes you have to have those skunk works things that even if the opinion-based leader doesn't like it, you're like, "That seems like the right thing. Maybe not right now, but it's going You can see it on the horizon." So you just keep working on those things. So but we had to like I said, the the iPhone wasn't a hit right away. It was kind of like a oh yeah, it worked on AT&T and it was 2 and a half G and it was this and that and you know, it did it only worked in the US. So, we had to get multiple versions till we got out and that's in my book, I have the and it's called a three I think it's three generations. Everything needs three generations as Bill there it is. Three generations. I've I've learned you make the product, you fix the product and then you fix the business. So, you have there's no I I've never seen anyone get it all right the first time. Like you want you would like to, but you get close, but make the product, fix the product after you get customer feedback and then make the business which means make the margins. Like on the first iPods we weren't making any money. First iPhones we weren't making any money. Right? The second iPhone or I say and second iPod, okay, we start getting a little bit better numbers. But we got more or less the features sort of dialed. Third one was like, okay, Windows, we got the margins, we're getting up the volume, we got all the right and like go. Right? Reliability, whatever else it had to be. So, you need to stick with your idea even if it's not necessarily going the first time unless there's something really severely your brain damaged about whatever it was. You have to restart, but sometimes you have to hang in there.
turns.zh: 嗯，你知道吗，iPod 一开始也不够大。它花了三代才成功。任何记得 iPhone 之前 iPod 的人都知道，iPhone 像黑洞一样吞噬了一切。但第一代 iPod 只对 Mac 极客有吸引力，而 Mac 极客不到市场的 1%。第二代也是这样。我们第一季能卖的都卖了，然后销量就死了，因为只有 Mac 发烧友、忠实用户会来买。直到第三代，我们让它兼容 Windows，加上 iTunes 音乐商店，它才真正起飞。所以有时候你必须说，我们方向对了，但需要做些改变来启动市场。那是一个基于意见的决策。我领导的团队很清楚，我们必须从一开始就支持 Windows 连接，或者至少在第一代 iPod 发货后马上支持。但 Steve 说：“除非我死，绝不可能。这会帮助我们卖更多 Mac。”然后我们做第二代时，还是只支持 Mac，我们想只要修好一些问题，它就会火起来。但并没有。后来我们通过一个秘密项目最终实现了 Windows 连接，这是个很长的故事。我在触控笔上也做了同样的事。Steve 从不想在 iPhone 或 iPad 上要触控笔。他说：“手指就够了。如果我们不能用手指完成，那就算了。”我说：“但我们在 B2B 场景中需要填表、写字。”他说：“我不在乎，那会变成 Windows 笔。”他担心会像 Windows 笔那样，所有操作都得用笔，而不是手指。他希望以手指为主，而不是笔。但后来我们通过另一个秘密项目加入了触控笔，它最终成为产品的一大特色。不是每个人都用，但对某些专业人士、艺术家和爱好者来说很重要。所以有时候你需要这些秘密项目，即使意见领袖不喜欢，你也觉得那是对的，也许不是现在，但你能看到未来。所以你就继续做。就像我说的，iPhone 也不是一炮而红。它只在 AT&T 上工作，是 2.5G，只在美国能用。我们出了好几个版本才成功。在我的书里，我提到三代法则：一切都需要三代。我学到的是：先做产品，然后根据客户反馈修正产品，最后修正业务，也就是改善利润率。第一代 iPod 和 iPhone 都没赚钱。第二代开始数字好一点，功能也基本调好了。第三代，有了 Windows，利润率上来了，销量也上来了，可靠性也好了。所以你需要坚持你的想法，即使第一次不成功，除非有根本性的缺陷，否则你得重新开始，但有时候你得坚持下去。

transcript.secZh: 当创意不够好：成功阈值

transcript.sec: Learning from failure and iteration

turns.en: And same thing with Nest, we had to hang in there with two generations of the smoke detector and a few generations of the thermostat before we made the business work. Right? Not just make the product work.
turns.zh: Nest 也是一样，我们坚持了两代烟雾报警器和几代恒温器，才让业务运转起来，对吧？不仅仅是让产品好用。

turns.en: There's so many directions I can go here. One that's interesting is how often Steve Jobs was actually wrong. And how many times you had to...
turns.zh: 这里有很多方向可以聊。有意思的一点是，史蒂夫·乔布斯其实经常犯错。你有多少次不得不……

turns.en: You're like takes a year still like, okay, finally... We all were wrong at certain times, but you know, it's when you hit it big, when you get the right ones, they overshadow all the other stuff. But that's how you have to try and iterate. Jeff Bezos says the same thing. I believe in the same thing. You got to fail a few times till you find your way. And you only fail if you stop. If you keep iterating and keep going, well, then that's not failure. That's called learning.
turns.zh: 你会觉得花了一年时间，终于……我们都有犯错的时候，但你知道，当你大获成功、做对了的时候，那些成功会盖过其他一切。但这就是你必须尝试和迭代的方式。杰夫·贝佐斯也这么说。我也相信这一点。你得失败几次才能找到方向。只有停下来才算失败。如果你不断迭代、继续前进，那就不叫失败，那叫学习。

transcript.sec: The iPod's role in saving Apple

turns.en: The other interesting part of the story you just shared is one of my favorite stories in your book, actually. The fact you implied this, but basically the first iPod was you had to have a Mac to use it. And the idea was it would sell Macs. This is a way to get people to buy Macs. And the way you tell it, this ended up saving Apple eventually. Not selling Macs, but people buying iPods. And that became the big part of the business.
turns.zh: 你刚才分享的故事里另一个有趣的部分，其实是我在你书中最喜欢的故事之一。你暗示了一点，但基本上第一代 iPod 必须用 Mac 才能使用。当时的想法是，这会带动 Mac 的销售，这是一种让人们购买 Mac 的方式。但按你的说法，这最终拯救了苹果——不是靠卖 Mac，而是人们买 iPod。这成了业务的主要部分。

turns.en: Yeah, the mantra was, Steve, if we don't have Windows connectivity, the iPod doesn't cost $349. It costs $3,000 because you got to buy a Mac. And you got to move all your digital life over to it. People are not about to take a risk on this company that's almost bankrupt for $3,000. So, how are we going to do that? Like, okay, let's make sure it is only $349 and that they can try it. And once they try the brand, they go, "Ooh, that's pretty interesting. Maybe I should try other products from this company and give them a shot." Because that was just such a sublime experience. And that's how it all changed. And that's how iPhone was able to be created, because without the iPod, I'm pretty sure there would have been no iPhone. There would probably have been no Apple. Because it was close to bankruptcy.
turns.zh: 是的，当时的信条是：史蒂夫，如果我们不支持 Windows，iPod 就不是 349 美元，而是 3000 美元，因为你得买一台 Mac，还得把整个数字生活迁移过去。人们不会为了一个濒临破产的公司冒 3000 美元的风险。那我们该怎么办？好吧，确保它只要 349 美元，让他们能试试。一旦他们尝试了这个品牌，就会觉得：“哦，这挺有意思的。也许我应该试试这家公司的其他产品，给他们一个机会。”因为那是一种非常美妙的体验。一切就这样改变了。iPhone 之所以能诞生，是因为没有 iPod，我敢肯定就不会有 iPhone。可能连苹果都不存在了，因为它当时濒临破产。

turns.en: Wow. That's crazy how close it was to that if some of these decisions went the wrong way.
turns.zh: 哇，真疯狂，如果这些决定中有些走错了方向，它离破产就那么近。

turns.en: Yeah, very close. Remember there was no retail back then, Apple retailers, none of that stuff. Most people don't know how on the ropes Apple was back in 2001.
turns.zh: 是的，非常接近。记住，当时没有零售店，没有苹果零售店，什么都没有。大多数人不知道 2001 年的苹果有多么岌岌可危。

transcript.sec: Customer journey and marketing beyond product

turns.en: So many stories, but I want to come back to the customer journey thread that you touched on, which I think is really important for people listening to this podcast to hear. Something that you reinforce again and again in the book, and you have this awesome image that we're going to show of the entire customer journey. Your point is that so many product builders focus on building the product and think build the best product and we're going to win. And you have this very important advice that there's so much more to it. Talk about especially the marketing piece. Talk about what you think builders don't really get even though they hear this advice in many ways.
turns.zh: 故事很多，但我想回到你提到的客户旅程这条线索，我认为这对收听本播客的听众来说非常重要。你在书中一再强调这一点，而且你有一张很棒的图，我们会展示整个客户旅程。你的观点是，很多产品构建者只关注打造产品，认为做出最好的产品就能赢。而你有一个非常重要的建议：远不止如此。请特别谈谈营销部分。谈谈你认为构建者即使多次听到这个建议，也未必真正理解的地方。

turns.en: When we build products, we define products when we build them. We have a good sense of the context when we are defining those things. We're living in that world. And so we're like, "Oh, we're like this and these are our issues and this is who we are." Maybe you put on personas. We would strip out and figure out who our target customers were with their personas. Is it a single mom with kids, or a dual income no kid family, or senior citizens, or whatever. We would come up with these personas and kind of live inside them as we're thinking about the product. But you have to also remember that these people don't live in that context. They live in their context, but they're not aware of your product in their context. So you have to bring it home to them and meet them where they are. Your marketing, your website, your Instagram ads, your earned and owned media, you really need to put your product in their context and make the visuals and the words sing to them. If not, they're not going to get it. Too many times we just say, "Only if we just make the perfect product." No, you have to put all, for a consumer thing specifically, and it's also really good for a B2B product as well. Make sure you understand your customer. When you say the right words, they go, "Oh, they get me. I want to listen to these more." This is before they ever got the product. You need to get them to convert in some way, and they need to hear that you've already thought about their issues, or you're living in their shoes, and they're like, "Yes. Yes. Yes, more of that." Is this an emotional point? Is this a rational point? You're weaving this tapestry to get them to come to some kind of trial or purchase or conversion. The best way to do that is word of mouth, by other early adopters. But you got to do the same thing for the early adopters who other people trust to talk to, because a lot of other people who are late adopters, they know, "Oh, that's wacky George. He tries everything." But if so-and-so's tried it, "Oh, I should take notice." What you want to do is make sure you understand the gestation of your customer and where they are. I also updated the Crossing the Chasm graphic in the book, saying how we go about this in our product developments. You really have to think about who your target user set is and your target marketing based on the version of product you're in and how you're going to get those higher and higher volumes. It really means speaking the context. Maybe when you first ship, you can speak to those early adopters, and you can put it in enough words that they're going to convert. But then the second version of the product, you're getting these later adopters, and you've got to speak in their language. And the third set of adopters, who are really laggards, you've got to really speak to them. It could be totally different. This was a very interesting story that was so emblazoned in my mind.
turns.zh: 当我们构建产品时，我们在定义产品。我们对定义时的背景有很好的感知。我们活在那个世界里。所以我们会想：“哦，我们就是这样，这些是我们的问题，这就是我们。”也许你会套用人物画像。我们会提炼出目标客户及其画像。是带孩子的单亲妈妈，还是双收入无子女家庭，还是老年人，等等。我们会想出这些画像，并在思考产品时设身处地。但你也必须记住，这些人并不活在那个背景里。他们活在自己的背景中，但在他们的背景里，他们并不知道你的产品。所以你必须把产品带到他们面前，在他们所在的地方与他们相遇。你的营销、网站、Instagram 广告、你的赢取和自有媒体，你真的需要把产品放在他们的背景中，让视觉和文字对他们产生共鸣。否则，他们不会理解。很多时候我们只说：“只要我们做出完美的产品就行了。”不，你必须把所有东西都放进去，特别是对于消费品，对 B2B 产品也非常好。确保你理解你的客户。当你说出正确的话时，他们会想：“哦，他们懂我。我想多听听他们的。”这发生在他们拿到产品之前。你需要让他们以某种方式转化，他们需要听到你已经考虑过他们的问题，或者你设身处地为他们着想，然后他们会说：“是的。是的。是的，再多来点。”这是情感点还是理性点？你在编织一幅画卷，引导他们进行某种试用、购买或转化。最好的方式是通过口碑，由其他早期采用者来传播。但你也需要对那些其他人信任的早期采用者做同样的事，因为很多后期采用者知道：“哦，那是古怪的乔治，他什么都试。”但如果某某人试过了，“哦，那我该注意了。”你要做的是确保你理解客户的孕育过程以及他们所处的位置。我还在书中更新了《跨越鸿沟》的图表，说明我们在产品开发中如何做到这一点。你真的必须思考你的目标用户群是什么，以及基于你产品的版本和如何获得越来越高的销量，你的目标营销是什么。这实际上意味着要讲背景。也许当你首次发布时，你可以对早期采用者说话，用足够的话让他们转化。但到了产品的第二个版本，你面对的是后期采用者，你必须用他们的语言说话。而第三批采用者，真正的落后者，你必须真正对他们说话。这可能完全不同。这是一个非常有趣的故事，深深印在我的脑海里。

transcript.secZh: 超越产品的客户旅程与营销

transcript.sec: Marketing Adaptation for New Markets

turns.en: Back in 2001 to 2003, Apple still had no presence outside the US. Apple was really selling in the US, some Canada, and some Japan. But that was it, and that was the Macs. So we were selling iPods into those areas. We had the early adopters, and then we had some other language, and by the third or fourth version of iPod, we had some really refined language for those late adopters. So we said, "We're going to go back and get a push into Europe." Because iPod hadn't taken off in Europe. So we were going to push iPod into Europe. We were on the fourth generation or something like that. So what did we do in Europe? We ran the same marketing as we were doing in the US in Europe. And it didn't resonate with the early adopters. It didn't resonate with the later adopters because we didn't have the same messages. We were going for a different set of people. And we were like, "Ding ding ding, sales aren't working." We said, "We got to change the marketing. We got to meet those Europeans where they're at because they're slower to adopt technology versus it starts with the coast and goes inward in the US, right?" So we were like, "Oh, we got to change things up." Sometimes when you get to new areas, you have to rethink your marketing for that area and remember to meet them where they're at. If it doesn't have an installed base for word of mouth, you're going to have to get that word of mouth started. So work with those people to get that going. Now it's different with all software products and many products can go global right away, but marketing you still got to tell a story. It just reminds me that even though we may compress the time for some pieces of the adoption puzzle because we can distribute faster, it doesn't mean the awareness can get accelerated and that we can get people to intelligently understand this product faster if we just say the words of somebody who might have known about the product for three years.
turns.zh: 回到 2001 年到 2003 年，苹果在美国以外还没有任何存在。苹果真的只在美国、加拿大和日本销售。但仅此而已，而且那是 Mac。所以我们把 iPod 卖到这些地区。我们有早期采用者，然后我们有一些其他语言，到第三或第四代 iPod 时，我们为那些晚期采用者准备了非常精炼的语言。所以我们说：“我们要回去，向欧洲推进。”因为 iPod 在欧洲还没有流行起来。所以我们打算把 iPod 推向欧洲。我们当时大概是第四代产品。那么我们在欧洲做了什么？我们在欧洲使用了和美国一样的营销策略。结果它没有引起早期采用者的共鸣，也没有引起晚期采用者的共鸣，因为我们的信息不同。我们针对的是不同的人群。我们意识到：“叮叮叮，销售不奏效。”我们说：“我们必须改变营销策略。我们必须迎合欧洲人的现状，因为他们采用技术的速度较慢，而在美国，技术是从沿海向内陆扩散的，对吧？”所以我们想：“哦，我们必须做出改变。”有时候当你进入新领域时，你必须重新思考该领域的营销策略，并记住要迎合他们的现状。如果没有口碑基础，你就得启动口碑传播。所以要和那些人合作来推动它。现在，所有软件产品和许多产品都可以立即全球化，但营销仍然需要讲故事。这提醒我，尽管我们可能压缩了采用拼图中某些部分的时间，因为我们可以更快地分发，但这并不意味着认知度可以加速，也不意味着如果我们只是重复那些可能已经了解产品三年的人的话，就能让人们更快地智能理解这个产品。

transcript.sec: The Story Behind '1000 Songs in Your Pocket'

turns.en: I got to ask about maybe the most famous tagline of all time when a product was launched: "A thousand songs in your pocket." Is there a story behind that?
turns.zh: 我必须问一下，也许是有史以来最著名的产品发布标语：“一千首歌装进口袋。”这背后有什么故事吗？

turns.en: Back in the day at Apple, and I think it's still that maybe it's changed now, but back in the Steve days, there were different functions of Apple. Because they were all lean, because the company was only 4,000 or 5,000 people, and it was already doing Macs and everything, each function was very functional. So the marketing didn't get into the engineering and the design folks. It was separate. Steve was the hub, and he was in each of them putting them together, like I said, the opinion-based decision-maker. So when we heard the tagline for the first time, I was like, "That's genius." Now, how that happened, what was behind the scenes? We were just a tiny team getting this done in 10 months. So everybody was just running for it, right? Versus iPhone, which was a two-and-a-half-year development, and we were in some discussions: should we call it the iPod phone or should we call it the iPhone, because Cisco had the iPhone back in the day and there were copyright and trademark issues. So we were into that because it was betting the farm and we were going to cannibalize iPod, right? So there was a lot more discussions. turns.zh: 在苹果的早期，我想现在可能已经变了，但在史蒂夫时代，苹果有不同的职能部门。因为公司只有四五千人，而且已经在做 Mac 和其他所有产品，每个部门都非常专业化。所以营销部门不接触工程和设计人员。它们是分开的。史蒂夫是中心，他把它们整合在一起，就像我说的，他是基于意见的决策者。所以当我们第一次听到这个标语时，我想：“这太天才了。”那么，它是怎么来的，幕后发生了什么？我们只是一个小团队，在 10 个月内完成了这件事。所以每个人都在拼命工作，对吧？相比之下，iPhone 的开发历时两年半，我们有过一些讨论：应该叫它 iPod phone 还是 iPhone，因为思科当时有 iPhone，存在版权和商标问题。所以我们陷入了这些讨论，因为这是押上全部身家，而且我们要蚕食 iPod，对吧？所以讨论要多得多。但在“一千首歌”的早期，每个人都在奔跑。实际上，不到 9 个月，真的只有 5 到 6 个月就完成了所有事情，因为我们实际上是在 4 月份开始的，10 月底就发货了。

transcript.sec: AI code generation and technical debt

turns.en: It's un... And they're like, "Well, the AI knows it." But when you think about it and you have to maintain it, you can have an agent make code for you and it could work and it could test, but is it secure? Is it maintainable? If there's something going wrong, can you roll things back and understand what's going on? There are so many other aspects to writing code and delivering a product that you still need humans in the loop. So when I think of product design, I think of software code with AI. And when you look at AI, if it's not architecting things and not segmenting things and looking at each of those things like I was saying, there are software architects, software optimizers, and just general coders. And then there are security reviews. If you don't have that mixture of experts around the code structuring it so that subsequent generations can get better and better, it just devolves into a mass of things you don't know. You're getting short-term gain for very long-term loss, and that's called software debt, right? Technical debt. And everybody hates technical debt. So you might be fixing something, but it most likely is giving more technical debt, especially at the high level. So now, how does that map to product management? If you're a product manager and you type this in and get some result, but you don't have a really good marketer, a really good marketing communications person, a really good architect, a really good manufacturing manager—all these different things in this thing you're getting—foundation and people think, "Well, my AI's going to be smarter." It's not proven to do that. What's proven is if you properly architect it and have co-pilot code go into certain subsegments or have co-pilot help you build the architecture, you modify, refine it, lock it in, and say just work on these few things, on these more limited scope things, yeah, you can make that work. I think that's the way we have to consider how we use these tools in product management capabilities as well. To just say it's all abstracted away, it's all going to be better, and when you ask specific pointed questions and you want this thing fixed in your marketing thing or whatever, and they're going to be like, "Well, the AI should just figure that out too." It's like you have given up so much. Sure you can write code, but there's going to be the difference between H&M and a luxury brand. You can get certain things that look like that and copies, but it doesn't last more than one washing or one season, and you throw it away, it's cheap. Or you go to the luxury thing and pay more, it's crafted, handcrafted, and it's going to be around for a while. There is this dichotomy: fast and throwaway. So, what's called fast fashion, we have fast software. But software, if you're going to build a real company, can't be throwaway. Maybe it can, but I don't think it can be, because you get technical debt and you have to start over again. So you've got to really understand how you're using these tools. A lot of these things that these AI coders can do, agent coders can do, can make you incredible prototypes. Do more prototypes. Do more of those things to help you get that informed gut to say we're going this direction, architect that in, and then work on the subsegments below it and all the expert systems that you need in each of those domains.
turns.zh: 嗯……他们就会说：“AI 知道怎么做。”但当你仔细想想，你需要维护它，你可以让智能体帮你写代码，它可能能运行、能测试，但它安全吗？可维护吗？如果出了问题，你能回滚并理解发生了什么吗？编写代码和交付产品还有很多其他方面，仍然需要人类参与。所以当我想到产品设计时，我想到的是带 AI 的软件代码。当你审视 AI，如果它没有做架构设计、没有做细分、没有像我说的那样审视每个部分——有软件架构师、软件优化师、普通程序员，还有安全审查——如果你没有这些专家组合围绕代码进行结构化，让后续迭代越来越好，它就会退化成一堆你不知道的东西。你是在用短期收益换取长期损失，这就是软件债务，对吧？技术债务。每个人都讨厌技术债务。所以你也许在修复某些东西，但很可能是在增加更多技术债务，尤其是在高层。那么，这如何映射到产品管理呢？如果你是一个产品经理，你输入一些东西得到结果，但你没有一个好的市场人员、好的营销传播人员、好的架构师、好的制造经理——所有这些不同角色——人们会想：“我的 AI 会更聪明。”但并没有证据证明这一点。真正被证明的是，如果你恰当地架构，让 Co-Pilot 代码进入某些子领域，或者让 Co-Pilot 帮你构建架构，你修改、完善、锁定它，然后说只在这几个有限范围内工作，那么是的，你可以让它工作。我认为这就是我们在产品管理能力中考虑如何使用这些工具的方式。如果说一切都抽象掉了，一切都会更好，当你提出具体问题、想在营销方面修复某个东西时，他们会说：“AI 应该也能搞定。”那你放弃得太多了。当然你可以写代码，但就像 H&M 和奢侈品牌的区别。你可以买到看起来像的东西和仿制品，但它洗一次或一季就坏了，然后扔掉，很便宜。或者你去买奢侈品，花更多钱，它是精心制作的、手工打造的，能用很久。这就是一种二分法：快速和一次性。所以，有了快时尚，我们也有了快软件。但如果你要建立一家真正的公司，软件不能是一次性的。也许可以，但我认为不行，因为你会积累技术债务，然后不得不重来。所以你必须真正理解如何使用这些工具。这些 AI 编码器、智能体编码器能做很多事情，可以帮你做出不可思议的原型。多做原型，多做这些事情，帮助你获得那种有依据的直觉，说我们往这个方向走，架构好，然后在下面的子领域工作，以及每个领域需要的所有专家系统。

turns.en: Yeah, and I think what you may also be saying here, which I think is really important, is that because it's so easy to build, people can just build all these features and additions, and the value accrues to build something awesome, and the product mind becomes more important to help push this stuff from just a sloppy every feature, every checkbox to something awesome that people actually use.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为你在这里可能也在说一个非常重要的事情：因为构建变得如此容易，人们可以随意添加各种功能和附加项，而价值在于构建出真正棒的东西，产品思维变得更加重要，帮助把这些东西从粗制滥造的每个功能、每个复选框，变成人们真正使用的出色产品。

turns.en: Because at the end of the day, if you're building it for yourself, whatever, go have fun. But if you're going to build it and you want to sell it, and you only need three key features to sell it to someone, it's going to have to be boiled down. It's going to have to be figured out because you're still selling it to a human who needs to understand it. Now, do you think you could vibe code Flighty?
turns.zh: 因为归根结底，如果你是给自己做的，那随便玩。但如果你要做出来并卖出去，而且只需要三个关键功能就能卖给某人，那就必须精简。必须想清楚，因为你仍然是在卖给需要理解它的人。那么，你觉得你能用 vibe coding 做出 Flighty 吗？

turns.en: Mhm. Probably.
turns.zh: 嗯。可能吧。

turns.en: Maybe now that Flighty exists, you could.
turns.zh: 也许现在 Flighty 已经存在了，你可以。

turns.en: Copy that.
turns.zh: 复制它。

turns.en: Like, you could do version two of Flighty, maybe vibe code it because you say, "Look at Flighty." But the original Flighty? That's luxury software. Right? It was understood how the pixels are done. So, you've got to remember, is it version one or is it version two, three, four? Because an opinion-based thing on highly innovative, differentiated stuff, it doesn't have a model for that. There's not that stuff in there. You still need the very version one to be done. So maybe you can prototype with these things. I could go on for hours, but that's just one old guy's point of view.
turns.zh: 比如，你可以做 Flighty 的第二版，也许用 vibe coding，因为你说“看看 Flighty”。但最初的 Flighty？那是奢侈品软件。对吧？它知道像素是怎么做的。所以你要记住，是版本一还是版本二、三、四？因为对于高度创新、差异化的东西，基于观点的东西，它没有模型。里面没有那些东西。你仍然需要做出第一个版本。所以也许你可以用这些东西做原型。我可以讲上几个小时，但这只是一个老家伙的观点。

turns.en: I think it's really important this insight that as it becomes easier to build, the things that stand out are the things that are really well thought through and great and luxury, almost as you described.
turns.zh: 我认为这个洞察非常重要：随着构建变得更容易，脱颖而出的东西是那些经过深思熟虑、出色且奢侈的，几乎就像你描述的那样。

turns.en: And you can feel it, right? And you go, "Oh my god." I'm one of the biggest proponents of telling them, "Flighty, Flighty." Because all the people in my sphere, they're all flying. I'm like, "Have you tried it? It's insane." So you get word of mouth and things take off because there has been that level of care and craft. And maybe a lot of the sub functions of Flighty could be built from co-pilot code or whatever, but the whole thing and the architecture, I don't think so.
turns.zh: 你能感觉到，对吧？你会说：“天哪。”我是最推崇告诉别人“Flighty，Flighty”的人之一。因为我圈子里的人都在飞。我会问：“你试过吗？太疯狂了。”所以口碑传播，事情就火了，因为那种程度的用心和工艺。也许 Flighty 的很多子功能可以用 Co-Pilot 代码或其他工具构建，但整体和架构，我不这么认为。

turns.en: And if someone hasn't tried Flighty, clearly download it and play with it. I love that this is the example of an amazing product. That's a really cool product.
turns.zh: 如果有人还没试过 Flighty，赶紧下载玩玩。我很喜欢这个作为出色产品的例子。那真是一个很酷的产品。

turns.en: All software. It's all software.
turns.zh: 所有软件。都是软件。

transcript.sec: The power of storytelling in product building

turns.en: Yeah. Something along these lines. As people hear you talk, clearly you are an amazing storyteller. And in your book, you often come back to the power of storytelling, the value of storytelling for product builders. What's, I guess one is just why is that so important, do you think? And two is what's like one tip you could give people to get better at storytelling?
turns.zh: 是的。顺着这个思路。听你说话，显然你是一个了不起的故事讲述者。在你的书里，你经常回到讲故事的力量，讲故事对产品构建者的价值。我想，第一是你认为为什么它如此重要？第二是你有什么建议可以让人们更好地讲故事？

turns.en: Storytelling, that's how we passed information down or got people to commit to doing something.
turns.zh: 讲故事，这是我们传递信息或让人们承诺做某事的方式。

transcript.secZh: 产品构建中讲故事的力量

transcript.sec: The Power of Storytelling

turns.en: Stories are so who we are. We go to the movies for stories. We have books. We have all this stuff. And it's just so essential to who we are because we like to be taken on a journey. We like to, and hopefully when you're buying a product or licensing one or subscribing, you're taking on a journey that meets your expectations or is better than what the expectations were set. It's much outsized that. So, Dave Chappelle, when it comes to comedy and storytelling, the way he weaves a story for 20 minutes to get to the punchline and you're just in it, right? I just love his comedy as opposed to the punchline guys who are extremely short. It's something in our nature, human nature. We were told stories when we were a kid, we read the same or watched the same movies a hundred times when we were kids. There's something about being taken on that journey that we love. And people love to be educated that way. Right? Your best college professors, high school teachers, they taught you why you should love certain math or certain physics and took you on a journey of why it mattered and then you're like, oh, now I get it. You can learn all the basics of how to do something. But that doesn't really tie it into something that's meaningful. So when you tie it to something that's human, that's when it becomes accessible for humans and it's relatable. That's when it goes and it can be for anything. And that's when great marketing, great sales happens. And great storytelling through the product design, that's even better, right? Because it wasn't just perfume on a pig and you put some on this bad product and you can say, oh yeah, try it and then it doesn't meet the expectations. But when it sings from the depths of the product, like you were bringing up with the Nest Protect, the thermostat or the smoke detectors. When we had all that and you could feel the love and care, that's when people go, oh, I love this and I want more from that brand or from that company or that team. That's what they want. So to get better at it, I've learned it because I watched my dad, he was in sales. I watched how he would sell Levi's. And he wasn't always selling to sell. Sometimes he was convincing them not to buy something because that wasn't the best product they had. Buy this one instead. And actually go to my competitor down the street because he was building a relationship and story time because these people loved what it was. So sometimes it's not just setting expectations and telling a story, but it's also saying, "Hey, maybe I'm not for you." And that's truth. That's also truth. So, like Steve always said, "The best marketing just tells the truth." Now, might put nice words around it, nice creative and everything, but it's telling the truth. So, when I watched Steve prepare for the iPhone, like I said, we did the 2 and a half years with the iPhone, he was honing the story of the iPhone every day. He didn't give it to marketing. He knew what that thing was and what those key features were, like we talked about, and micromanage those features because he knew those were the things the world was going to take notice of. And he would refine the story and then tell other people who were unwatched by, like friends who are really smart, and say, "I'm going to give you the pitch." And he would refine, refine, refine. So when you saw him come on stage, it was just because he had done it 100,000 times or at least 10,000 times, right? And he knew it like boom, boom, boom, boom, boom. It just came off. So I watched and I learned from that. And that's what we did for the Nest Learning Thermostat. That's what we learned, we did for each of those things. It was just that same storytelling over and over. What does it matter? Why does it matter? Why does it matter? Because too many times when we're technology-led, we talk about the what. We don't talk about the why. And the why is where the storytelling is. Because you want to take the journey of why it matters to you. And if you're talking about the what, you're just talking to another geek. Love geeks, no problem. I'm one of them. But most people who are not in the technology world, they want a story and they want something that touches them in some way.
turns.zh: 故事就是我们本身。我们去看电影是为了故事，我们有书，有所有这些。故事对我们来说至关重要，因为我们喜欢被带入一段旅程。我们希望，当你购买产品、授权或订阅时，你踏上的旅程能符合甚至超越你的预期。它远超预期。所以，戴夫·查普尔，在喜剧和讲故事方面，他编织故事的方式，用 20 分钟铺垫一个笑点，而你完全沉浸其中，对吧？我就喜欢他的喜剧，而不是那些只讲短笑话的人。这是我们天性中的一部分，人性。我们小时候听故事，读同一本书或看同一部电影上百遍。被带入一段旅程的感觉让我们着迷。人们也喜欢通过这种方式学习，对吧？你最好的大学教授、高中老师，他们教你为什么应该喜欢数学或物理，带你踏上探索其重要性的旅程，然后你恍然大悟，哦，现在我懂了。你可以学习如何做某事的基础知识，但那并不能将其与有意义的东西联系起来。所以当你把它与人性联系起来时，它才变得易于理解和 relatable。这时它才能适用于任何事物，伟大的营销和销售才会发生。通过产品设计讲好故事甚至更好，对吧？因为那不是在劣质产品上喷香水，然后说“试试吧”，结果达不到预期。但当产品本身就能打动人心时，就像你提到的 Nest Protect、恒温器或烟雾报警器。当我们做到那一点，人们能感受到其中的关爱和用心时，他们就会说，哦，我喜欢这个，我想要更多来自这个品牌、公司或团队的东西。这就是他们想要的。为了做得更好，我学会了这一点，因为我观察我父亲，他做销售。我看他如何卖 Levi's。他并不总是为了卖而卖。有时他会说服顾客不要买某样东西，因为那不是最好的产品。他会说“买这个吧”，甚至建议去街对面的竞争对手那里，因为他是在建立关系和故事时间，因为这些人热爱它。所以有时不仅仅是设定预期和讲故事，还要说“嘿，也许我不适合你”。那是真相。那也是真相。就像史蒂夫常说的，“最好的营销就是讲真话。”现在，可能会用漂亮的词句和创意来包装，但本质是讲真话。所以，当我看到史蒂夫为 iPhone 做准备时，就像我说的，我们花了两年半时间，他每天都在打磨 iPhone 的故事。他没有交给营销部门。他知道那是什么以及关键功能是什么，就像我们讨论过的，他亲自管理这些功能，因为他知道这些是世界会注意到的。他会不断打磨故事，然后讲给其他人听，比如那些非常聪明的朋友，说“我要给你做演示”。然后他不断打磨、打磨、再打磨。所以当你看到他上台时，那是因为他已经练习了 10 万次，至少 1 万次，对吧？他烂熟于心，一气呵成。所以我观察并学习。这就是我们为 Nest Learning Thermostat 所做的。我们为每个产品都这样做了。就是反复讲同一个故事。它有什么意义？为什么重要？为什么重要？因为很多时候，当我们以技术为主导时，我们谈论的是“是什么”，而不是“为什么”。而“为什么”才是讲故事的核心。因为你希望踏上这段旅程，了解它为什么对你重要。如果你只谈“是什么”，那你只是在和另一个极客对话。我爱极客，没问题，我也是其中一员。但大多数非技术领域的人，他们想要一个故事，想要一些能触动他们的东西。

turns.en: One of the lessons I'm hearing from what you just described is telling the story over and over and over and refining it over time is where the best stuff comes from. Not just, here's I just came up with this and it's going to be amazing.
turns.zh: 我从你刚才的描述中学到的一点是，反复讲述故事并不断打磨，才能产生最好的东西。而不是“我刚想到这个，它一定会很棒”。

turns.en: Yeah, oh absolutely. Because you need to tell it to other people who don't know to see if it resonates. I remember the number of times I told the Nest story before Nest came out because I was like, "This is crazy, this thermostat thing." I'm like, "Okay, let me set up the virus of doubt which was: Do you know how much you spend on your energy bill every year for your heating and cooling? Don't you hate your thing?" Now, there's another way to look at the story. And I put this in the book. Oh, I don't know if I put this in the book. We all have seen infomercials. Right? Those that drag on for half an hour, an hour on late night TV, on some random channel. And they just sit there and tell you the story from all different directions. They give you the virus of doubt. They're like, "Oh, here's this cheese grater. And when you have this bad cheese grater, your knuckles bleed and it's hard to clean," and they over exaggerate everything and show you all the pain points. And then they show how wonderful and easy it is to use this whatever they're selling. And then it's easy to buy and easy to return and it's overly dramatic. And we all know it's like wrestling. It's all an act. But it is storytelling in a way. And not that you should do that because they overhype the expectations, but look at the techniques that are used, the psychological techniques, the emotional techniques to get there.
turns.zh: 是的，完全正确。因为你需要讲给不了解的人听，看看是否能引起共鸣。我记得在 Nest 发布之前，我讲了多少次 Nest 的故事，因为我想，“这个恒温器太疯狂了。”我说，“好吧，让我先种下怀疑的种子：你知道你每年在取暖和制冷上花多少钱吗？你难道不讨厌你的恒温器吗？”现在，还有另一种讲故事的方式。我把这个写进了书里。哦，我不知道有没有写进去。我们都看过电视购物广告，对吧？那些在深夜电视上拖沓半小时、一小时的广告，在某个随机频道上。他们就坐在那里，从各个角度给你讲故事。他们给你种下怀疑的种子。他们会说，“哦，这是一个奶酪刨丝器。当你用这个糟糕的奶酪刨丝器时，你的指关节会流血，而且很难清洗，”他们夸大一切，展示所有痛点。然后展示他们卖的东西多么好用、多么简单。然后购买方便、退货容易，而且非常夸张。我们都知道这就像摔跤，全是表演。但这在某种程度上也是讲故事。不是说你应该那样做，因为他们过度炒作预期，但可以看看他们使用的技巧，心理技巧和情感技巧。

transcript.sec: Marketing as caricature of truth

turns.en: And then dial it back and go, "Okay, I'm going to do that but with truth." And that's when things start to go pop pop pop pop. Or at least that's what I've seen. And so I always use that as another metric. And it's not just telling the story over and over, it's what story you are telling. And so I try to think of that as well. But not I obviously clowny and cheesy and everything else, but it is a caricature of doing good marketing.
turns.zh: 然后往回拉，说：“好吧，我要那样做，但要真实。”这时候事情就开始噼里啪啦地爆发了。至少我观察到的是这样。所以我一直把它当作另一个衡量标准。而且这不只是反复讲同一个故事，而是你讲的是什么故事。所以我也会考虑这一点。当然不是那种滑稽、俗气之类的，但它是做好营销的一种夸张表现。

turns.en: I feel like this is a really interesting lens into the press release first or working backwards ideas, create the infomercial first and just go to extreme.
turns.zh: 我觉得这是一个非常有趣的视角，先写新闻稿或者逆向思维，先做电视购物广告，然后做到极致。

turns.en: Sure, you could.
turns.zh: 当然可以。

turns.en: Oh my god.
turns.zh: 天哪。

turns.en: No, what you don't want to do for sure.
turns.zh: 不，你肯定不想那样做。

turns.en: But it's like a cool lens to go really far and then kind of pull back the best parts and make it honest. I think we just came up with a really good idea here.
turns.zh: 但这就像一个很酷的视角，先走得很远，然后把最好的部分拉回来，让它变得诚实。我觉得我们刚刚想到了一个很好的主意。

transcript.sec: Vision for the next iPhone with AI

turns.en: I want to come back to the iPhone. So I think it came out like 20 years ago almost at this point, right? It launched 2007 something like that.
turns.zh: 我想回到 iPhone 的话题。我觉得它差不多已经问世 20 年了，对吧？大概是 2007 年发布的。

turns.en: So what's crazy is people are still trying to build the next iPhone, especially with the AI. Everyone's just like, what's We've had this thing forever. What the hell? Like hasn't anything changed? I'm curious. I imagine people ask you this question. I'm curious just like what do you think the next iPhone may look like with AI? What's like the device that you think emerges over time? Do you have a vision?
turns.zh: 疯狂的是，人们还在试图打造下一个 iPhone，尤其是在 AI 方面。每个人都在想，我们一直有这个设备，到底怎么回事？难道什么都没变吗？我很好奇。我想人们会问你这个问题。我很好奇，你觉得 AI 加持的下一个 iPhone 会是什么样子？你认为随着时间的推移会出现什么样的设备？你有愿景吗？

turns.en: Okay, there's the what it could look like long-term and there's what it could look like soon. And so a lot of people like, "Okay, there's the long-term thing and when we can trust the models and when they have memory and when they do this stuff, then we're still going to need a display cuz sorry people, unless we're plugging it into our brain like a BCI brain computer or there's some laser thing going into our retina, we're going to need a display. So barring those kinds of technologies, we're going to need a display. And so the best display that we have is a smartphone-like thing, okay? It's not going to be this tiny thing. We saw what happened with Humane and all that other stuff. So you're going to have some kind of cuz the best way to visualize visual information is with a display, right? So, we're going to have some kind of small slab, maybe it's foldable like we see today, whatever, so that you can access it. You maybe many things you don't need a display for, but a lot of things you still do, right? Because you might not be tapping and swiping and all that stuff. So, I am of the opinion that long term, if you look at how a device is layered today, and this is many, many devices, and iPhone specifically started this, which was, you know, tapping and swiping. Right? That was the first thing is, you know, you use your finger. Then after that was keyboard. And then after that, the tertiary thing was a voice input. We need to flip it. We need to absolutely flip it, and we have to say, and this is what I always wanted to do with Nest, which is I want to remove displays, and we need to have voice as the number one primary feature, and you build around voice. Then we have keyboard if necessary, and then we have tapping and swiping. Okay? It should go exactly the opposite. turns.zh: 好吧，有长期可能的样子，也有近期可能的样子。很多人说：“长期来看，当我们能信任模型，当它们有记忆，能做这些事情时，我们仍然需要一块屏幕，因为抱歉，除非我们把东西插进大脑，比如脑机接口，或者有某种激光投射到视网膜上，否则我们需要一块屏幕。”所以排除这些技术，我们还需要屏幕。而我们最好的屏幕就是类似智能手机的东西，对吧？它不会是很小的东西。我们看到了 Humane 和其他类似产品的下场。所以你会需要某种小方块，也许是像现在这样的折叠屏，这样你就能访问信息。很多事情你不需要屏幕，但很多事情你仍然需要，对吧？因为你可能不需要点触和滑动之类的操作。所以我的观点是，长期来看，如果你看今天设备的层次，很多设备，尤其是 iPhone 开创的，就是点触和滑动，对吧？那是第一层，你用你的手指。然后是键盘。再之后是语音输入。我们需要翻转这个顺序，彻底翻转。我们必须说，这也是我在 Nest 一直想做的，我想去掉屏幕，让语音成为首要功能，围绕语音构建。然后如果需要键盘，再然后是点触和滑动。应该完全反过来。问题在于，我们大多数人不会对着手机说话，我们在汽车上也看到了这一点：有物理按钮，然后有触摸屏，然后也加入了语音，但除了无障碍场景，几乎没人用语音，对吧？因为语音总是最后添加的，它只是勉强能用，是个小玩意儿，就像 Alexa 1.0 或 Siri 那样。但当我们真正拥有非常好的语音输入时，不仅像 WhisperFlow 那样能理解，而且背后有智能、有记忆等等，那时我们才能开始更具体地使用它，并逐步淘汰其他交互方式。但我们一直需要把它们作为语音的拐杖，因为语音从未真正可靠过。所以这是长期的情况。它会有某种屏幕，但语音会是首要的，其他是次要和第三位的。在中期或不久的将来，它看起来会很像智能手机，因为我们不会很快摆脱全应用界面。我们会移动一些东西，但我们还不信任它。需要一段时间才能信任。我们实际上是把很多控制权交给这个可信的东西，对吧？因为点触和滑动，我们基本知道那是什么。我们知道点触和键盘输入时我们可以信任自己。但这个东西我们不知道。所以需要很长时间。这让我想起 General Magic，我们需要大众信任它。我们已经看到编码智能体之类的情况，比如“哦，他们删了我的源代码”。我不是在说它行不通，它当然在某些事情上可行。我是说对于消费者日常想要的东西，而且它要足够便宜。今天人们说：“我要试试 ChatGPT，每月 20 美元或 200 美元。”如果认为消费者会付这个钱，那是不可持续的。除非它非常出色。但他们不会，你明白吗？他们试了，然后很多人觉得这就像 Siri 1.0。我付了钱，但它并没有那么好。就像全自动驾驶，我付了全自动驾驶的钱，15 年过去了，我还在等。所以我认为我们再次理解了我们以为自己想要什么，但技术现状、社会采用和需要建立的社会信任，都需要很长时间和很多迭代才能实现。尤其是如果你还得为此付费。

transcript.sec: Cycles of hardware and software innovation

turns.en: I don't even know. I wanted to ask also, it's interesting that basically everyone is getting into hardware now. Like, you've been at this for so long building hardware in AI, and now it's just like the hottest thing. Everyone is building hardware. So funny. Thoughts? I don't know. How does that feel?
turns.zh: 我也不知道。我还想问，有趣的是现在基本上每个人都在做硬件。你已经在 AI 硬件领域深耕了这么久，而现在这成了最热门的事。每个人都在造硬件。真有意思。你怎么看？我不知道。你感觉如何？

turns.en: Look, I was building hardware when it wasn't in vogue in 1995 and 96. Everyone was like, "Tony, you're crazy. It's all about the internet. We don't need any hardware." And then the iPod comes. I was pitching new businesses in '99 and 2000. They're like, "Well, that's the stupidest idea ever." Then the iPod comes out. "Tony, you want to leave Apple and start that business you wanted to do with hardware?" You know, and then it was like, "Yeah, okay." And then it was all software mobile stuff again. Like, "Okay, we don't need anything because we can't get to the next level of software if we don't make the next level of hardware." And the revolution has to happen completely. Like, you have to have the mobile network and the mobile network software for the mobile network to work. You had to have the MP3 player and the MP3 format bits to make that work. We're seeing with AI, we have to have AI plus all the data centers and edge compute to make that work. So over time, the hardware becomes more mundane. Like, "Okay, it doesn't change as much." But all the software starts to then, you know. So I've always just been continually going through the things that I love to do and doing things at the full stack level because that's where innovation is. That's what we had to do at Nest. We had to innovate at the software level, the hardware level, the network level to get the first thermostat out and then the Nest Protect and all that stuff. If we look now, people are like, "If you're a SaaS company or a software-only company, your software company's worthless because anyone can vibe code it into the thing." And now they're like, "We're only funding companies that have atoms in their business plan with software." I'm like, "Duh." Like, where have you guys been? So it's just funny to watch these cycles go and happen. It's like, "Okay, all right, you guys can go chase your tails. I'm going to just keep doing these full system kind of products and businesses." Yes, they're a lot harder and yes, they cost more money and yes, they're going to take longer to scale and adopt and blah blah blah, but they have staying power for years, right? And they bring new features that you could have never had if you just did software only, because you need, especially in robotics, new sensors and this and that, what have you, right? Look at Waymo. It's an electric car with tons of sensors and everything. It was a hardware platform. Maybe it doesn't look as streamlined as a smartphone, but it's an incredible hardware platform with an incredible software platform, and that is something that we're going to be able to innovate on, and it'll become a platform for something else, I'm sure. You know, delivery or whatever else.
turns.zh: 听着，我在 1995 年和 1996 年硬件还不流行的时候就开始做硬件了。每个人都觉得，“托尼，你疯了。现在都是互联网的天下，我们不需要任何硬件。”然后 iPod 来了。我在 99 年和 2000 年推销新业务时，他们都说，“这真是有史以来最蠢的主意。”然后 iPod 发布了。“托尼，你想离开苹果去创业做硬件吗？”然后我就说，“好啊，没问题。”接着又全是软件移动端的东西了。就像，“好吧，我们什么都不需要，因为如果我们不做下一代的硬件，就无法达到下一代的软件水平。”革命必须彻底发生。比如，你必须拥有移动网络和移动网络软件，移动网络才能工作。你必须拥有 MP3 播放器和 MP3 格式的比特流才能让它工作。我们看到 AI 也是如此，我们必须拥有 AI 加上所有的数据中心和边缘计算才能让它工作。所以随着时间的推移，硬件会变得更平凡。就像，“好吧，它变化没那么大了。”但所有的软件就开始……你知道的。所以我一直持续做我喜欢的事情，并且在全栈层面做事，因为那里才是创新的所在。我们在 Nest 就必须这样做。我们必须在软件层面、硬件层面、网络层面进行创新，才能推出第一款恒温器，然后是 Nest Protect 等等。现在再看，人们会说，“如果你是一家 SaaS 公司或纯软件公司，你的软件公司一文不值，因为任何人都可以用 vibe coding 把它做出来。”现在他们又说，“我们只投资那些商业计划中包含硬件和软件的公司。”我就想，“废话。”你们之前都去哪儿了？所以看着这些周期循环往复真的很有趣。就像，“好吧，你们可以去追自己的尾巴了。我继续做这些全系统类的产品和业务。”是的，它们更难，成本更高，需要更长时间来扩展和采用，等等等等，但它们有持久的生命力，对吧？而且它们带来了如果你只做软件永远无法实现的新功能，因为你需要，尤其是在机器人领域，新的传感器等等，对吧？看看 Waymo。它是一辆电动汽车，带有大量传感器和一切。它是一个硬件平台。也许它看起来不像智能手机那样流线型，但它是一个令人难以置信的硬件平台，配有一个令人难以置信的软件平台，我们将在其上进行创新，而且我确信它会成为其他东西的平台。比如配送或其他什么。
